posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked:
Cool, I could not find another note on WB.com with this old vine serine by Domaine Levet. Yea, lots of notes on the very delicious Les Journaries and the La Chavaroche.

The important stuff first: 100% serine from Cote Blonde, Cote Brune, Molard, Baleyat, Chavaroche, according to JLL at Drink Rhone. About 30-50% destemmed. Aged in 30% new and 70% used 600-liter oak casks, 24 months.

Borrowing JLL’s lexicon, a rather dark robe to this wine. Has the visual appeal of depth and ripeness. Archetype Levet nose of pungent stems, smoke and game. Clearly a ripe year with rich red and dark fruits, a damp mossy feel to the palate, crisp acid. Powerful, mouth-coating. With air and time, the dry and chewy tannins typical of the La Chav cuvée kick in hard, with a bit of a bitter astringency suggestive of some newish oak. This wine has huge potential but clearly needs to sort out the tannins and astringency, which with my experience on the La Chav, causes me no worries. Not really changed much on day two, perhaps more chalk to the tannins over astringency. This is a huge wine, clearly a powerful vintage, both ripe and structured. Has a bit of warmth to it, not heat, but definitely showing 13.5% alcohol. This is not a wine of finesse, it is rather medieval.

(91 pts.)

posts.name: Daniel Mattisson
posts.cooked: With the understanding that these need plenty of time before they come into their own, how would you rank the last 5 vintages (2010-2015) for Levet?

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked:
2010
2013
2012
2011
2014

Can’t really rank 2015 since I have not tried the two top cuvees, but this Amethyst suggests that Les Journaries and La Chav could slot out high. Still think 2010 is the best.

You didn’t ask, but 2004, 2005, 2007 and 2009 are really good to excellent as well.

posts.name: Markus S
posts.cooked:
That’s beyond ‘dark robe’, veers more toward 'impenetrable blackness.!

And still trying to figure out the ‘why’ behind new oak and syrah?

posts.name: Jason R o b e r t s o n
posts.cooked: Thanks so much for the note! I had wondered about this cuvée since it was a blend. I also wasn’t sure about destemming or not. I grabbed a couple of bottles and, based on your note, I might head back for more. You had me at medieval.
posts.display_username: Jason R o b e r t s o n

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked: Markus, I thought Levet always had used oak, but would need to review some prior vintage data to confirm. The presence of new oak on the mouthfeel was a bit of a surprise, didn’t know until I read JLL’s notes on 2015, that 30% new oak was used. Same thing for La Chav. Is this a departure from prior practices?

posts.name: John Morris
posts.cooked:
Robert Alfert:

Markus, I thought Levet always had used oak, but would need to review some prior vintage data to confirm. The presence of new oak on the mouthfeel was a bit of a surprise, didn’t know until I read JLL’s notes on 2015, that 30% new oak was used. Same thing for La Chav. Is this a departure from prior practices?

But 600-liter, not 225-liter, barrels, so there’s less surface area for the same volume of wine.

posts.name: Marcus Dean
posts.cooked:
Robert Alfert:

Markus, I thought Levet always had used oak, but would need to review some prior vintage data to confirm. The presence of new oak on the mouthfeel was a bit of a surprise, didn’t know until I read JLL’s notes on 2015, that 30% new oak was used. Same thing for La Chav. Is this a departure from prior practices?

I guess that you would need to replace oak casks at the end of their useful life,
Perhaps this was the year that they needed to replace some.

posts.name: Gerhard P.  (P r @ € s e n t)
posts.cooked:
Marcus Dean:

I guess that you would need to replace oak casks at the end of their useful life,
Perhaps this was the year that they needed to replace some.

Depends whether you long for the taste/smell of new oak - or not.
If not one can use these casks for ages - as is/was the practice e.g. at Henri Bonneau, Rayas, Gentaz-Dervieux et al.
I´ve seen casks that were 90+ years old …
posts.display_username: Gerhard P.  (P r @ € s e n t)

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
I don’t think is an old vine cuvée, or at least not exclusively.

My conversations chez Levet led me to think new oak was more 10-15% (compared to e.g. Jamet at 20-30% new), but it has been a few years, and obviously no one knows the regions more intimately than JLL. And yes, demi-muids.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Robert Alfert:

2010
2013
2012
2011
2014

Can’t really rank 2015 since I have not tried the two top cuvees, but this Amethyst suggests that Les Journaries and La Chav could slot out high. Still think 2010 is the best.

You didn’t ask, but 2004, 2005, 2007 and 2009 are really good to excellent as well.

And reason you omit 2006? I personally think it’s superb and one of the best recent vintages for current consumption if one must open bottles on the young side.

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked:
William Kelley:

I don’t think is an old vine cuvée, or at least not exclusively.

My conversations chez Levet led me to think new oak was more 10-15% (compared to e.g. Jamet at 20-30% new), but it has been a few years, and obviously no one knows the regions more intimately than JLL. And yes, demi-muids.

The distributor, Rosenthal, says 10-15% new but he’s had that reference on the site for a few years, so I would go with JLL. I do not subscribe, so do not have access to his notes on all years, would be curious about new oak on earlier vintages. This is the most obvious note of wood that I have ever picked up on a Levet. Not that it was bad, just apparent.

You are correct, the wine is a blend of old and young vines from various vineyards, including La Chavaroche.

I only left out 2006 in my listing as my memory is fading at my advanced young age of 52! Had it 2 years ago and it stood out even in this line-up:

Yes, it is mighty fine as well.

posts.name: Marcus Dean
posts.cooked:
Robert Alfert:

Markus, I thought Levet always had used oak, but would need to review some prior vintage data to confirm. The presence of new oak on the mouthfeel was a bit of a surprise, didn’t know until I read JLL’s notes on 2015, that 30% new oak was used. Same thing for La Chav. Is this a departure from prior practices?

I had a look at The JLL website this morning and he notes that the 2015 is as usual a multiplot blend of 100% syrah (doesnt mention old vine or serine in his description), that is vinified in concrete vats , desteemed 30% in this vintage and aged in 30% new and 70% used 600L casks and 30HL Barrels, half of the wine is aged in cask and half in Barrel.
So by my maths 15% of the vintage was exposed to new oak as per usual,and I presume is the same regime used at least since this label was created in 2006
I have heard mention in interviews with other traditional producers that some wines from this vintage appear to show oak on the palate even though no new oak was used in the elevage.
